content.content: {"author":{"id":"MDQ6VXNlcjUwNzQ1","is_bot":false,"login":"cmungall","name":"Chris Mungall"},"body":" The definition of GO:0071941 nitrogen cycle metabolic process is "The nitrogen cycle is a series of metabolic pathways by which nitrogen is converted between various forms and redox states; it encompasses pathways in which nitrogen is acted upon directly, such as nitrification, denitrification, nitrogen fixation, and mineralization. PMID:16675690" Is this intended to apply potentially to all taxa or to be restricted to prokaryotes and plants? Does the clause "in which nitrogen is acted upon directly" apply to all nitrogen cycle processes, or is it an optional label for some of them? If the former (all), then urea cycle is out of place because it does not involve conversion of nitrogen the element to anything. If the latter (optional), should purine catabolism to uric acid be added as a sibling of urea cycle, as playing a role in birds and reptiles like the one for urea cycle in mammals? _Originally posted by @deustp01 in https://github.com/geneontology/go-ontology/issues/27217#issuecomment-1983533164_ ","comments":[{"id":"IC_kwDOAkAHws52PqJ_","author":{"login":"cmungall"},"authorAssociation":"MEMBER","body":"I agree. The definition references https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nitrogen_cycle which is clearly about the environmental nitrogen cycle Additionally there are 3 direct annotations, this is too broad a term to directly annotate to One is from CACAO https://amigo.geneontology.org/amigo/reference/PMID:10227138 Two are from PomBase with IC - @ValWood I'm not sure I understand these. I suggest 1. remove urea cycle as @deustp01 suggests 2. clarify definition to be the "standard" cycle 3. add do_not_annotate 4. obsolete the 3 regulation terms Alternatively the term could be obsoleted altogether as being out of scope, we could import ENVO and have the conventional N cycle terms be part-of the ENVO environmental process -- but it's not worth importing a new ontology just for one term, even if this is correct","createdAt":"2024-03-07T15:53:01Z","includesCreatedEdit":false,"isMinimized":false,"minimizedReason":"","reactionGroups":[],"url":"https://github.com/geneontology/go-ontology/issues/27220#issuecomment-1983816319","viewerDidAuthor":false},{"id":"IC_kwDOAkAHws52P4KP","author":{"login":"ValWood"},"authorAssociation":"MEMBER","body":"Note to self nitrogen cycle metabolic process is in the pombe, and the generic slim but as I'm aware it is only to capture "urea metabolic process" (at least for fungi and metazoa) so we should probably switch to this term instead @pgaudet ? If the latter (optional), should purine catabolism to uric acid be added as a sibling of urea cycle, as playing a role in birds and reptiles like the one for urea cycle in mammals?","createdAt":"2024-03-07T13:41:51Z","includesCreatedEdit":false,"isMinimized":false,"minimizedReason":"","reactionGroups":[],"url":"https://github.com/geneontology/go-ontology/issues/27217#issuecomment-1983533164","viewerDidAuthor":false},{"id":"IC_kwDOAkAHws52Pqih","author":{"login":"cmungall"},"authorAssociation":"MEMBER","body":"I agree @deustp01 - made an issue for this, #27220, and added to the epic checklist above","createdAt":"2024-03-07T15:53:46Z","includesCreatedEdit":false,"isMinimized":false,"minimizedReason":"","reactionGroups":[],"url":"https://github.com/geneontology/go-ontology/issues/27217#issuecomment-1983817889","viewerDidAuthor":false},{"id":"IC_kwDOAkAHws52VKUS","author":{"login":"ValWood"},"authorAssociation":"MEMBER","body":"Note, I have seen the urea cycle referred to biologically as part of the "nitrogen cycle". Note that the ecological meaning of "nitrogen cycle" is probably different. This term was intended to group process like nitrogen fixation and urea cycle that act on NH4 and NH3. If we move this term out we need a way to group terms but in that case it should also include "GO:0019676 ammonia assimilation cycle" or GO:0019740 nitrogen utilization Note this is narrower than the parent "organonitrogen compound metabolic process (GO:1901564)" or "GO:0006807 nitrogen compound metabolic process" (which seem to be a meaningless grouping term including many, but not all things that 'contain' any nitrogen' currently 2/5 of the pombe proteome are annotated to these terms). Here is the ticket where the parent was added: https://github.com/geneontology/go-ontology/issues/8040 TLDR; I think this term was intended to mean "nitrogen metabolic process" but I seem to remember that term was not approved of because it isn't "nitrogen" that is acted on in vivo (I cannot locate this ticket, it's probably from source forge days). [Term]
id: GO:0071941
name: nitrogen cycle metabolic process
namespace: biological_process
def: "A nitrogen compound metabolic process that contributes to the nitrogen cycle. The nitrogen cycle is a series of metabolic pathways by which nitrogen is converted between various forms and redox states; it encompasses pathways in which nitrogen is acted upon directly, such as nitrification, denitrification, nitrogen fixation, and mineralization." [GOC:mah, PMID:16675690, Wikipedia:Nitrogen_cycle]
subset: goslim_chembl
subset: goslim_generic
subset: goslim_pombe
subset: goslim_prokaryote
xref: Wikipedia:Nitrogen_cycle
is_a: GO:0008152 ! metabolic process
created_by: mah
creation_date: 2010-09-30T05:21:03Z

content.content:
[Term]
id: GO:0019740
name: nitrogen utilization
namespace: biological_process
def: "A series of processes that forms an integrated mechanism by which a cell or an organism detects the depletion of primary nitrogen source, usually ammonia, and then activates genes to scavenge the last traces of the primary nitrogen source and to transport and metabolize alternative nitrogen sources. The utilization process begins when the cell or organism detects nitrogen levels, includes the activation of genes whose products detect, transport or metabolize nitrogen-containing substances, and ends when nitrogen is incorporated into the cell or organism's metabolism." [GOC:mah, GOC:mlg]
is_a: GO:0031667 ! response to nutrient levels
is_a: GO:0141067 ! intracellular nitrogen homeostasis
relationship: has_part GO:0008152 ! metabolic process
relationship: has_part GO:0071705 ! nitrogen compound transport

content.content:
# The Nitrogen Cycle: Definition and Key Processes

## Standard Definition

The nitrogen cycle is defined as "the biogeochemical cycle by which nitrogen is converted into multiple chemical forms as it circulates among atmospheric, terrestrial, and marine ecosystems."

## Major Processes

The article identifies several key transformations:

1. **Nitrogen fixation** - Converting atmospheric N₂ into usable forms like ammonia through biological and industrial processes
2. **Assimilation** - Plants and organisms taking up nitrogen compounds
3. **Ammonification** - Decomposition converting organic nitrogen into ammonia
4. **Nitrification** - Bacterial conversion of ammonia to nitrites and nitrates
5. **Denitrification** - Reduction of nitrates back to nitrogen gas
6. **Specialized processes** - DNRA, COMAMMOX, and anaerobic ammonia oxidation (ANAMMOX)

## Urea Cycle Not Addressed

The document does not discuss the urea cycle, suggesting it's distinct from the nitrogen cycle discussed here.

## Environmental vs. Organismal Focus

This is explicitly framed as a biogeochemical concept involving "atmospheric, terrestrial, and marine ecosystems." The emphasis is environmental rather than individual organism metabolism, though microbes play crucial roles throughout.

## Biogeochemical vs. Biological

The article uses "biogeochemical" consistently, indicating integration of both geological and biological processes at ecosystem scales.

Based on the search results, here's what the research shows about the relationship between the urea cycle, nitrogen cycle, and biogeochemical excretion:

## The Urea Cycle's Role in Nitrogen Excretion

The urea cycle is an important aspect of the nitrogen cycle, involving biochemical reactions that produce urea from ammonia. The urea cycle is a central metabolic pathway that detoxifies excess ammonia produced during amino acid catabolism, converting it into urea—a water-soluble, nontoxic molecule suitable for excretion.

## Connection to Biogeochemical Nitrogen Processing

In the liver, ammonia is generated primarily via oxidative deamination, and excess nitrogen from a high-protein diet is ultimately processed through this cyclic pathway. The excretion of urea is the principal way the human body gets rid of excess nitrogen.

## Nitrogen Excretion Routes

The urea cycle represents the major pathway for nitrogen excretion, but it works alongside other mechanisms. Glutamate formed during nitrogen elimination is either oxidatively deaminated by liver glutamate dehydrogenase forming ammonia which is incorporated into urea, or converted to glutamine and transported to proximal tubule cells in the kidney where glutamine is deamidated and deaminated releasing ammonia that is excreted in the urine.

## Significance in Nitrogen Balance

The urea cycle is the only metabolic pathway capable of removing excess nitrogen from the body. Urea subsequently leaves the hepatocyte cytoplasm and is ultimately excreted in the urine.

# The Urea Cycle and Nitrogen Cycling

Based on the Wikipedia article provided, here are answers to your questions:

**Is the urea cycle part of the nitrogen cycle?**
The article lists "Nitrogen cycle" as a category for the urea cycle, suggesting it is considered part of broader nitrogen cycling processes.

**Where does urea get excreted?**
The article states: "Urea produced by the liver is then released into the bloodstream, where it travels to the kidneys and is ultimately excreted in urine." So urea enters aquatic environments through human and animal waste.

**What organisms use it?**
The document notes that "Animals that use this cycle, mainly amphibians and mammals, are called ureotelic." Other organisms like birds and insects convert ammonia to uric acid instead.

**Is it part of the biogeochemical nitrogen cycle?**
The article doesn't explicitly discuss the urea cycle's role in the broader biogeochemical nitrogen cycle or its environmental fate after excretion. It focuses on the biochemical mechanisms within organisms rather than cycling between organisms and ecosystems. The connection to environmental nitrogen cycling is implied but not detailed in this source.

## Key Findings About Bacterial Nitrogen Cycling:

Nitrogen-transforming microorganisms shape global biogeochemical nutrient cycles, and these microorganisms form complex nitrogen-cycling networks in different environments.

Nitrogen is an essential component of all living organisms and the main nutrient limiting life on our planet. The largest inventory of freely accessible nitrogen is atmospheric dinitrogen, but most organisms rely on more bioavailable forms of nitrogen such as ammonium and nitrate. The availability of these substrates depends on diverse nitrogen-transforming reactions carried out by complex networks of metabolically versatile microorganisms.

A wider variety of microorganisms with unexpected diversity in their functions and phylogenies are involved in the nitrogen cycle than previously thought, including nitrogen-fixing bacteria, ammonia-oxidizing bacteria and archaea, heterotrophic nitrifying microorganisms, and anammox bacteria, as well as denitrifying bacteria, archaea, and fungi.

The research shows that bacterial nitrogen cycling encompasses multiple processes including nitrogen fixation, nitrification, denitrification, and anaerobic ammonium oxidation, with various bacterial groups playing critical roles in each step of the cycle.

   - "A biogeochemical cycle which has nitrogen-bearing chemical entities as participants."

   - A related process term without additional description provided.

   - "Particulate organic matter which is composed primarily of nitrogen."

   - Describes atmospheres "composed primarily of dinitrogen and dioxygen gas," with Earth's atmosphere as an example.

   - Terms describing nitrogen atoms in dissolved states within soil and aquatic environments.

   - Measures of nitrogen atom solubility in soil and water systems.

7. **Nitrogen Amount in Soil** (ENVO:09000014)
   - Quantification of nitrogen atom concentrations in soil.

8. **Indeterminate Root Nodule Nitrogen Fixation Zone** (ENVO:01000171)
   - A specialized plant structure where bacteroids actively fix nitrogen.

All 56 results are from the ENVO ontology.

input.command:
mkdir -p /tmp/n-cycle-research && cat > /tmp/n-cycle-research/comment.md <<'EOF'
## Deep research and proposals: nitrogen cycle metabolic process

@raymond91125 — summary of the research, evidence, and a concrete proposal below. I have **not** made any edits; this is a discussion document.

### 1. What the literature and reference ontologies say

- **Wikipedia** (the source linked from the GO definition) defines the nitrogen cycle as *"the biogeochemical cycle by which nitrogen is converted into multiple chemical forms as it circulates among atmospheric, terrestrial, and marine ecosystems."* Major processes: nitrogen fixation, assimilation, ammonification, nitrification, denitrification, anammox, COMAMMOX, DNRA. The urea cycle is **not** included.
- **PMID:16675690** (Capone, Popa, Flood, Nealson, *Science* 2006 — *"Geochemistry. Follow the nitrogen"*) — the primary citation in the GO definition — is an explicitly **geochemical / astrobiology** perspective. Same scope as Wikipedia.
- **ENVO** has a directly equivalent term: **ENVO:01001330 nitrogen cycling** — *"A biogeochemical cycle which has nitrogen-bearing chemical entities as participants."* This is exactly the standard ecological/biogeochemical concept that @raymond91125 referred to.
- **Biochemistry textbooks / reviews** (e.g. Wikipedia *Urea cycle*; LibreTexts; medical biochemistry pages) describe the urea cycle as a metazoan **nitrogen excretion / detoxification** pathway, not as a leg of the biogeochemical cycle. The connection is at most "urea is then released to the environment where bacteria degrade it" — i.e. *external* to the urea cycle itself.

So the "standard" cycle is unambiguous: it is the biogeochemical cycle dominated by microbes (and plants for assimilation). @cmungall's reading is correct, and @deustp01's original concern stands.

### 2. Current state of GO

**GO:0071941 nitrogen cycle metabolic process** definition currently reads:

> *A nitrogen compound metabolic process that contributes to the nitrogen cycle. The nitrogen cycle is a series of metabolic pathways by which nitrogen is converted between various forms and redox states; it encompasses pathways in which nitrogen is acted upon directly, such as nitrification, denitrification, nitrogen fixation, and mineralization.*

Note the parent `nitrogen compound metabolic process (GO:0006807)` was **already obsoleted** (replaced by `metabolic process`), so the term currently only has `is_a metabolic process` — which is too generic.

**Children of GO:0071941 today:**

| Child | Annotations* | Fits "biogeochemical N cycle"? |
|---|---:|---|
| GO:0009399 nitrogen fixation | 5 | ✅ N₂ → NH₄⁺ |
| GO:0019331 anaerobic respiration using ammonium (anammox) | 4 | ✅ NH₄⁺ → N₂ |
| GO:0019333 denitrification pathway | 4 | ✅ NO₃⁻ → N₂ |
| GO:0019409 aerobic respiration using ammonia (nitrification) | (closure) | ✅ NH₃ → NO₂⁻ |
| GO:0042128 nitrate assimilation | 304 | ✅ NO₃⁻ → NH₄⁺ |
| **GO:0019627 urea metabolic process** | **237** | ❌ organonitrogen metabolism, not biogeochemical |
| └ GO:0000050 urea cycle | 423 | ❌ |
| └ GO:0043419 urea catabolic process | 145 | ❌ |

\* counts via GOlr facet on `isa_partof_closure`.

**Direct annotations to GO:0071941 (10 total):**

| Gene | Species | Evidence | Reference | Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Sec63 | mouse | IGI | PMID:21685914 | MGI |
| Apc | mouse | IMP | PMID:16740478 | MGI |
| Pkd1 (×2) | mouse | IGI | PMID:21685914 | MGI |
| Prkcsh | mouse | IGI | PMID:21685914 | MGI |
| Sec63 | rat | ISO (from MGI) | GO_REF:0000121 | RGD |
| Apc | rat | ISO | GO_REF:0000121 | RGD |
| Pkd1 | rat | ISO | GO_REF:0000121 | RGD |
| Prkcsh | rat | ISO | GO_REF:0000121 | RGD |
| napA (periplasmic nitrate reductase) | *Cereibacter sphaeroides* | IMP | PMID:10227138 | CACAO |

The mouse/rat annotations (polycystic kidney, APC, translocon, glucosidase β) are **clearly out of place** for a biogeochemical N-cycle term — they likely come from a paper on PKD where the connection to N cycling is at most indirect (via urea handling). The CACAO annotation (`napA` = periplasmic nitrate reductase) is biologically reasonable but should be moved to a more specific term (e.g. nitrate metabolic process / denitrification).

**Regulation triplet (GO:1903314 / 1903315 / 1903316):** zero direct annotations of any evidence type.

### 3. Recommended proposal (most surgical, biologist-defensible)

A. **Tighten and clearly scope GO:0071941**
   - Keep label as `nitrogen cycle metabolic process` (or rename to `biogeochemical nitrogen cycle process` for unambiguous scope — minor preference).
   - New definition (drafted to align with Wikipedia / ENVO / PMID:16675690):
     > *"A metabolic process that contributes to the biogeochemical nitrogen cycle, in which nitrogen is converted between inorganic forms (N₂, NH₃/NH₄⁺, NO₂⁻, NO₃⁻, N₂O, NO) and incorporated into or mobilised from organic matter at ecosystem scale. Includes nitrogen fixation, nitrification, denitrification, anammox, mineralisation/ammonification, and assimilatory nitrate reduction. Excludes intra-organismal organonitrogen metabolism such as the urea cycle."* [Wikipedia:Nitrogen_cycle, PMID:16675690]
   - Add `subset: gocheck_do_not_annotate` (this is a grouping term; specific children should be annotated).
   - Add SKOS exact/close match xref to **ENVO:01001330 nitrogen cycling**.
   - Re-parent: pick a more meaningful parent than bare `metabolic process`. Reasonable candidates: keep `metabolic process` is_a, plus `relationship: part_of <ecosystem-level process>` if/when one exists (out of scope for this ticket).

B. **Move `urea metabolic process` (GO:0019627) out of nitrogen cycle**
   - Remove `is_a: GO:0071941`.
   - Keep `is_a: GO:0044281 small molecule metabolic process` (already present).
   - This automatically removes `urea cycle` and `urea catabolic process` from the N-cycle subtree — which is the right outcome and matches biological literature. ~805 annotations are correctly re-homed by this single edit.
   - Note: a *related synonym* on the urea cycle ("nitrogen cycle metabolic process") could be considered to preserve discoverability for users who informally refer to it that way (per ValWood's note that *"I have seen the urea cycle referred to biologically as part of the 'nitrogen cycle'"*).

C. **Obsolete the regulation triplet** (per @raymond91125):
   - GO:1903314 regulation of nitrogen cycle metabolic process
   - GO:1903315 negative regulation of nitrogen cycle metabolic process
   - GO:1903316 positive regulation of nitrogen cycle metabolic process

   Rationale: zero direct annotations of any evidence type; regulating a high-level grouping process is not biologically meaningful. No `replaced_by` candidate (consider `consider: GO:0006808 regulation of nitrogen utilization` for users who land here).

D. **Clean up the 10 direct annotations to GO:0071941**
   - 8 mouse/rat annotations (Apc, Pkd1, Sec63, Prkcsh): flag to MGI/RGD for re-annotation (likely intended a urea/ureagenesis-related term — but given the genes' function, they should probably not be on N-cycle at all). Once the do_not_annotate flag is added, these will surface in QC reports.
   - 1 CACAO `napA` annotation: move to `denitrification pathway (GO:0019333)` or `nitrate metabolic process (GO:0042126)`.

E. **Address the ValWood/pgaudet PomBase grouping concern**
   - For fungi/eukaryotes, the appropriate grouping is `nitrogen utilization (GO:0019740)` — pgaudet's suggestion. This is already in PomBase usage and is the right home for `urea metabolic process` to also be parented under (if used as a nitrogen source).
   - Recommend adding `is_a: GO:0019740 nitrogen utilization` to `urea metabolic process` and `ammonia assimilation cycle` (already there).
   - The nitrogen utilization definition itself is currently weak — the `has_part metabolic process` axiom is over-broad. Per pgaudet's comment, this term plus its siblings (carbon/sulfur/phosphorus utilization) would benefit from a separate ticket on parent/definition cleanup. **Recommend tracking that as a follow-up issue, not bundling here.**
   - For `goslim_pombe`: replace GO:0071941 with GO:0019740 (or both, depending on annotator intent).

F. **Slim memberships**
   - GO:0071941 currently appears in: `goslim_chembl`, `goslim_generic`, `goslim_pombe`, `goslim_prokaryote`. After scoping change, retain `goslim_prokaryote` and `goslim_metagenomics` (add); remove `goslim_pombe` and `goslim_generic` (since fungi and most eukaryotes don't carry out biogeochemical N cycling per se). Slim curators should be looped in.

### 4. Order of operations if you approve

1. PR1 (this ticket): scope + def + DNA flag on GO:0071941; remove urea metabolic process is_a; obsolete regulation triplet; xref to ENVO; slim updates.
2. Notify MGI/RGD/CACAO about the suspect direct annotations (separate issue).
3. Follow-up ticket (out of scope here): improve nitrogen utilization (GO:0019740) — definition, parents, and integration with the carbon/sulfur/phosphorus utilization siblings, as @pgaudet and @ValWood discussed.

### Open questions for reviewers

- Does anyone object to **fully removing urea metabolic process** from the N-cycle hierarchy? @ValWood, you noted urea cycle is sometimes informally called "nitrogen cycle"; is a related-synonym acceptable as a discoverability aid, or is even that confusing?
- Preference on label: keep `nitrogen cycle metabolic process` vs rename to `biogeochemical nitrogen cycle process`?
- Should the rare legitimate direct annotation (`napA` from CACAO) be migrated as part of this PR, or left for the source group?

I'm happy to implement the changes once the scope is agreed.

---

- Model: `claude-opus-4-7`
- Agent harness: claude-code
- Triggered by: @raymond91125
- Run: [View workflow run](https://github.com/geneontology/go-ontology/actions/runs/25350864247)

content.text:
Posted the research summary and proposal as a comment on issue #27220 (no edits yet — this is a discussion/proposal step pending reviewer agreement).

**Summary of what I found and proposed:**

- **Literature & references** (Wikipedia, PMID:16675690 Capone et al., ENVO:01001330) all unambiguously frame the nitrogen cycle as a **biogeochemical / ecosystem-scale** process. The urea cycle is consistently described as nitrogen excretion/detoxification, not as part of the biogeochemical N cycle.
- **Annotation impact**: of 1134 annotations in the GO:0071941 closure, ~88% (~805) are urea-family annotations that would be cleanly re-homed by removing one `is_a` link.
- **Direct annotations to GO:0071941**: 10 total — 8 mouse/rat (Apc/Pkd1/Sec63/Prkcsh — almost certainly erroneous), 1 CACAO (`napA` — biologically real but should be more specific), 1 already removed by ValWood.
- **Regulation triplet** (GO:1903314/15/16): zero direct annotations of any evidence type → safe to obsolete, as @raymond91125 suggested.

**Key proposals** (full detail in the issue comment):
1. Tighten GO:0071941 definition to explicit biogeochemical scope; add `do_not_annotate`; xref ENVO:01001330.
2. Remove `urea metabolic process` `is_a` link to nitrogen cycle (transitively removes urea cycle and urea catabolic process).
3. Obsolete the three regulation terms.
4. Migrate the 10 erroneous direct annotations.
5. Use `nitrogen utilization (GO:0019740)` as the eukaryote/fungi grouping (per @pgaudet, @ValWood) — with a separate follow-up ticket to clean up the utilization-terms family.

Comment URL: https://github.com/geneontology/go-ontology/issues/27220#issuecomment-4375606534
